The present survey of Vergilian scholarship is the eighteenth in the Vergilius series. Classical
World 68:1
(1974), published in association with the
Vergilian Society,
Inc., contains my 'Recent Work on Vergil:
A
Bibliographical
Survey, 1964-73'. The survey has been reprinted in The Classical
World Bibliography of Vergil (Walter Donlan, ed.), Garland Publishing, Inc.
(New York & London, 1978). Subsequent surveys have appeared in Vergilius
19 (1973), 20 (1974), 21 (1975), 22 (1976), 23 (1977), 24 (1978) and 25
(1979).
Several noteworthy collections are contained inthe 1979-80 Bibliography.
One, edited by Raymond Chevallier, and entitled Présence de Virgile: Actes
du Colloque des 9, 11, 12 Décembre, 1976 (Paris 1978), appears repeatedly
and is abbreviated throughout as Présence. A. J.Boyles' thematic number of
Ramus 8:1 (1979) is entitled Vergil's Ascraean Song: Ramus Essays on the
Georgics; it appears repeatedly and is abbreviated throughout as Virgil's Ascraean Song. Four noteworthy bibliographies are awaiting publication inthe
already monumental ANRW: 'A Bibliography of Virgil's Eclogues' by Ward
W.
Briggs,
Jr.; and three additional bibliographies, all compiled by Werner
Suerbaum (Munich): 'Eine systematische Arbeitsbibliographie mit besonderer
Berücksichtigung der Aeneis'' 'Spezialbibliographie zu Vergils Geórgica '; and
'Von der Vita
Vergiliana
über die Accessus Vergiliani zum Zauberer Vergilianus: Probleme - Perspektiven - Analysen'. The ANRW bibliographies differin their chronological
limits:
Briggs continues his bibliography of the
Eclogues to 1977; Suerbaum closes his listings with 1975. Classical World will
publish my cumulative 'Recent Work on Vergil, 1974-1982' during the Bimillenium in1982.
Off prints of articles and bibliographic notices for inclusion in the annual
Vergilius review are always welcomed by the editor. Address correspondence
to Professor A. G. McKay, Department of Classics, McMaster University,
Hamilton, Ontario, Canada L8S 4M2.
1.

to
Vergilius l-XXV,'
Vergilius 25 (1979)
87-95.

C. Nordenfalk (ed.), Vergilius Aureus. Codex Vaticanus Latinus 3256
der Bibiiotheca apostolica Vaticana und Codex Latinus fol. 416 der Staatsbibliothek Preussischer Kulturbesitz (Graz 1976).
Paul Alpers' verse translation of the Eclogues is everything one might expect from a Professor of English, contemporary and lucid, more attractive
than Boyles' recent version (Melbourne 1976). The Delille reprint is artistically attractive. Eggenberger's analysis of the Vergilius Romanus miniatures is
iconographical and stylistic; the script is dated between A.D. 590 and 610.
Joly retraces well-worn paths with
useful comments. Lebek examines Aen.
1.683ff. ina discussion of epigrammatic lines at Pompeii.

der Bibiiotheca apostolica Vaticana und Codex Latinus fol. 416 der Staatsbibliothek Preussischer Kulturbesitz (Graz 1976).
Paul Alpers' verse translation of the Eclogues is everything one might expect from a Professor of English, contemporary and lucid, more attractive
than Boyles' recent version (Melbourne 1976). The Delille reprint is artistically attractive. Eggenberger's analysis of the Vergilius Romanus miniatures is
iconographical and stylistic; the script is dated between A.D. 590 and 610.
Joly retraces well-worn paths with
useful comments. Lebek examines Aen.
1.683ff. ina discussion of epigrammatic lines at Pompeii.

30.
R. D.Williams
(ed.),
Virgil:
The Eclogues and Georgics (New York
1979).
Paul Alpers' fresh reading of the bucolic poems accents the way the aesthetic concerns and pleasures of the poems derive from emotional, ethical, and
social realities. His interpretations of the poems apply to later works in the
same genre and to issues of poetics ingeneral. Coleiro 's life work (487 pages)
contains general considerations, critical essays on the several eclogues (3, 2, 5,
7, 9 & 1, 6, 4, 8, and 10), and text with apparatus criticus. The apparatus will
excite greatest
interest and comment. Division into three sections enables
Monsignor Coleiro to deal with readings from the main MSS which demand
serious attention and study; to record MSS readings which are more or less
obvious mistakes but which aid in the reconstruction of the history of the
text; and finally, to furnish evidence of ancient testimonia and of scholarship
throughout the ages to our own time. Coleiro provides an unusual bird's-eye
view of the history of the scholarship of the
text of the Bucolics. Thill
suggests
that Tityrus is indebted to Theocritus' Komatas (Idyll 7). Deryck
Williams' new text of the Eclogues and Georgics is marked by acute scholarship, uncanny sensitivity to sound and metre, and astute 'timely' judgment.
Scholars and
teachers will
be
forever in his debt: Macmillan has found a
worthy successor to the inimitable Page.

raw_text:
G. Benzer, Einige Ansätze struktura!istischen Interpretierens. Mit. Beitr.
zu
Vergil, Eel.
VII; Horazjamb. II und c. I, 9; Tibull /,6 (Diss. Innsbruck,
1976).
Du Quesnay provides a paradigm of scholarship
in his study of creative
imitation in Eclogue 2. For him, as for his enlightened reader, the 'constant
interplay between model and imitation becomes a source of intellectual and
aesthetic pleasure' (p. 68). The relationship with Idyll 11
is virtually omnipresent, with Idyll 3 and other sources percolating alongside with lesser impetus. Manson points to the Augustan concern with children, evident inCatullian epithalamia and in Vergil's enigmatic Fourth Eclogue. Nethercut counters
Duckworth (ТАРА 89. 1958. 1-8) who transposed verse 23 to follow verse 20
and argues for the preservation of verses 18-25. Turcan refers to Servius, ad
Вис. 5.29 to account for the identification of Caesar with Daphnis in EcL 5.
The ambiguity of EcL 6.24
is interpreted by Egan as satis est quod potui
videri. Thomas' article underscores Vergil's interest inword play.

G. Benzer, Einige Ansätze struktura!istischen Interpretierens. Mit. Beitr.
zu

VII; Horazjamb. II und c. I, 9; Tibull /,6 (Diss. Innsbruck,
1976).
Du Quesnay provides a paradigm of scholarship
in his study of creative
imitation in Eclogue 2. For him, as for his enlightened reader, the 'constant
interplay between model and imitation becomes a source of intellectual and
aesthetic pleasure' (p. 68). The relationship with Idyll 11
is virtually omnipresent, with Idyll 3 and other sources percolating alongside with lesser impetus. Manson points to the Augustan concern with children, evident inCatullian epithalamia and in Vergil's enigmatic Fourth Eclogue. Nethercut counters
Duckworth (ТАРА 89. 1958. 1-8) who transposed verse 23 to follow verse 20
and argues for the preservation of verses 18-25. Turcan refers to Servius, ad
Вис. 5.29 to account for the identification of Caesar with Daphnis in EcL 5.
The ambiguity of EcL 6.24
is interpreted by Egan as satis est quod potui
videri. Thomas' article underscores Vergil's interest inword play.

raw_text:
Dorothea Wender, 'From Flesiod to Homer by way of Rome,' Virgil's
Ascraean Song 59-64.
Betensky examines the
imagery of war in the Georgics where
warfare
against nature is both a metaphorical ideal and an intrusive reality. Her final
remarks concern the farmer's life as the lone continuity, threatened yet surviving, but always on the brink between Golden Age and disaster. Boyle is
concerned
with
the poem's politico-historical frame. Ambivalence, veering
towards pessimism, is pervasive and tragic. Human impotence, vulnerability,
dissolution and failure; brutishness, pitilessness, injustice (or amorality) are
endemic aspects of the mortal world. There are darker tones to the Laudes
Ita/iae. Boyle's essay, contained inthe Ramus Collection which he has edited,
is a 'bravura' exercise aimed at discovering the nature and meaning of the
enigmatic Georgics. Peter Connor argues that Vergil uses description to work
on the reader's imagination with the goal of creating a knowledge which is
not mere knowing but fresh attitudes. Vergil's most mature examination of
otium, for P. J. Davis,
is to be found in the Georgics. Patricia Johnston's
study of the agricultural golden age, inter alia, shows how the bees of G. 4
become
the realization of the nova progenies of E. 4. Vergil's deliberate
contrast between Aristaeus and Orpheus marks the displacement of the pastoral by the agricultural ideal. The healing role of Apollo assists the passage to
this more complex stage of civilization. Gretchen Kromer discusses the didactic convention and the assumptions underlying its use exemplified for Vergil
by
Lucretius' De Rerum Natura.
She studies the didactic language
in the
Georgics with emphasis on the poet's narrative or rhetorical stance and argues
that Vergil used the convention to examine and question the basic assumptions of the genre. Gary Miles views the Georgics as a case-study of efforts by
contemporary Romans to respond to chaotic late Republican times, when
idealizations of country life and 'reactionary' attempts to recover the agrarian
past were abroad. Miles' perceptive study
treats the poem as a series of
meditations on several visions of agrarian life. Frances Muecke's study of G. 2
is an insightful study of the self-conscious aspects of Vergil's didactic poem.
She provides detailed analyses of such passages in the Georgics. The poem
finds its moral and poetic justification
in transforming Lucretius' search for
individual happiness into a search for the well-being of society. O'Loughlin
sees the Georgics as more than a hymn to the work ethic. Vergil's poem is a
celebration of labor as a necessary concomitant of festive otium. Dorothea
Wender's essay relates the Georgics to its literary predecessors and provides a
list (p. 60) of literary borrowings or reminiscences inthe Georgics as basis for
her brief discussion. The progress is from Hesiodic tradition to Epicureanism
(G. 1, 2), thence to the tough-minded impersonal view of Homer ( G. 3, 4).
60
of Civic and Retired Leisure. The Traditions of Homer and Vergi/,
Horace,
and Montaigne. (Chicago & London 1978). Rev: Charles Fantazzi, CW 73
(1979-80) 254; T. G. Rosenmeyer, Phoenix 33 (1979) 184-6.

title: Betensky examines the imagery of war in the Georgics where warfare against nature is both a metaphorical ideal and an intrusive reality. Her final remarks concern the farmer's life as the lone continuity, threatened yet surviving, but always on the brink between Golden Age and disaster. Boyle is concerned with the poem's politico-historical frame. Ambivalence, veering towards pessimism, is pervasive and tragic. Human impotence, vulnerability, dissolution and failure; brutishness, pitilessness, injustice (or amorality) are endemic aspects of the mortal world. There are darker tones to the Laudes Ita/iae. Boyle's essay, contained inthe Ramus Collection which he has edited, is a 'bravura' exercise aimed at discovering the nature and meaning of the enigmatic Georgics. Peter Connor argues that Vergil uses description to work on the reader's imagination with the goal of creating a knowledge which is not mere knowing but fresh attitudes. Vergil's most mature examination of otium, for P. J. Davis, is to be found in the Georgics. Patricia Johnston's study of the agricultural golden age, inter alia, shows how the bees of G. 4 become the realization of the nova progenies of E. 4. Vergil's deliberate contrast between Aristaeus and Orpheus marks the displacement of the pastoral by the agricultural ideal. The healing role of Apollo assists the passage to this more complex stage of civilization. Gretchen Kromer discusses the didactic convention and the assumptions underlying its use exemplified for Vergil by Lucretius' De Rerum Natura. She studies the didactic language in the Georgics with emphasis on the poet's narrative or rhetorical stance and argues that Vergil used the convention to examine and question the basic assumptions of the genre. Gary Miles views the Georgics as a case-study of efforts by contemporary Romans to respond to chaotic late Republican times, when idealizations of country life and 'reactionary' attempts to recover the agrarian past were abroad. Miles' perceptive study treats the poem as a series of meditations on several visions of agrarian life. Frances Muecke's study of G. 2 is an insightful study of the self-conscious aspects of Vergil's didactic poem. She provides detailed analyses of such passages in the Georgics. The poem finds its moral and poetic justification in transforming Lucretius' search for individual happiness into a search for the well-being of society. O'Loughlin sees the Georgics as more than a hymn to the work ethic. Vergil's poem is a celebration of labor as a necessary concomitant of festive otium. Dorothea Wender's essay relates the Georgics to its literary predecessors and provides a list (p. 60) of literary borrowings or reminiscences inthe Georgics as basis for her brief discussion. that Vergil used the convention to examine and question the basic assump-
tions of the genre. Gary Miles views the Georgics as a case-study of efforts by
contemporary Romans to respond to chaotic late Republican times, when
idealizations of country life and 'reactionary' attempts to recover the agrarian
past were abroad. Miles' perceptive study
treats the poem as a series of
meditations on several visions of agrarian life. Frances Muecke's study of G. 2
is an insightful study of the self-conscious aspects of Vergil's didactic poem.
She provides detailed analyses of such passages in the Georgics. The poem
finds its moral and poetic justification
in transforming Lucretius' search for
individual happiness into a search for the well-being of society. O'Loughlin
sees the Georgics as more than a hymn to the work ethic. Vergil's poem is a
celebration of labor as a necessary concomitant of festive otium. Dorothea
Wender's essay relates the Georgics to its literary predecessors and provides a
list (p. 60) of literary borrowings or reminiscences inthe Georgics as basis for
her brief discussion. The progress is from Hesiodic tradition to Epicureanism
(G. 1, 2), thence to the tough-minded impersonal view of Homer ( G. 3, 4).

title: Betensky examines the imagery of war in the Georgics where warfare against nature is both a metaphorical ideal and an intrusive reality. Her final remarks concern the farmer's life as the lone continuity, threatened yet surviving, but always on the brink between Golden Age and disaster. Boyle is concerned with the poem's politico-historical frame. Ambivalence, veering towards pessimism, is pervasive and tragic. Human impotence, vulnerability, dissolution and failure; brutishness, pitilessness, injustice (or amorality) are endemic aspects of the mortal world. There are darker tones to the Laudes Ita/iae. Boyle's essay, contained inthe Ramus Collection which he has edited, is a 'bravura' exercise aimed at discovering the nature and meaning of the enigmatic Georgics. Peter Connor argues that Vergil uses description to work on the reader's imagination with the goal of creating a knowledge which is not mere knowing but fresh attitudes. Vergil's most mature examination of otium, for P. J. Davis, is to be found in the Georgics. Patricia Johnston's study of the agricultural golden age, inter alia, shows how the bees of G. 4 become the realization of the nova progenies of E. 4. Vergil's deliberate contrast between Aristaeus and Orpheus marks the displacement of the pastoral by the agricultural ideal. The healing role of Apollo assists the passage to this more complex stage of civilization. Gretchen Kromer discusses the didactic convention and the assumptions underlying its use exemplified for Vergil by Lucretius' De Rerum Natura. She studies the didactic language in the Georgics with emphasis on the poet's narrative or rhetorical stance and argues that Vergil used the convention to examine and question the basic assumptions of the genre. Gary Miles views the Georgics as a case-study of efforts by contemporary Romans to respond to chaotic late Republican times, when idealizations of country life and 'reactionary' attempts to recover the agrarian past were abroad. Miles' perceptive study treats the poem as a series of meditations on several visions of agrarian life. Frances Muecke's study of G. 2 is an insightful study of the self-conscious aspects of Vergil's didactic poem. She provides detailed analyses of such passages in the Georgics. The poem finds its moral and poetic justification in transforming Lucretius' search for individual happiness into a search for the well-being of society. O'Loughlin sees the Georgics as more than a hymn to the work ethic. Vergil's poem is a celebration of labor as a necessary concomitant of festive otium. Dorothea Wender's essay relates the Georgics to its literary predecessors and provides a list (p. 60) of literary borrowings or reminiscences inthe Georgics as basis for her brief discussion. raw_text:
Aya Betensky, 'The Bees and the Cyclops in the Fourth Geòrgie
Vergilius 25 (1979) 26 31.
Ward Briggs offers a succinct, perceptive resumé of amor in G. 3, and the
Aeneid. West's contribution analyses the Vergilian plague in light of Lucretius1 horror in Athens. Lucretius shows more concern for accurate description
of observable phenomena, following Thucydides; Vergil, though indebted to
Lucretius, exhibits characteristic traits: rhetorical elaboration, emotion, anthropomorphism, religious asides, and moralizing. His tendency is to write for
effect and lose
sight of the
truth!
Betensky examines G. 4.149-227 and
studies aspects in which bees and Cyclopes reflect one another: birth, habitat,
autonomy, immortality, products, and control of violence. A series of anitheses summarizes the comparison of bees and Cyclopes: bees/Cyclopes =
above earth/ below earth; mobile/stationary; autonomous/dependent; mortal
/immortal; honey/thunderbolts;
special
farming/ special technology. 'The
logic of daily life, preoccupied with prescribing techniques and enumerating
species, gives way to the logic of mythology, which compares the essence of
things.' They inhabit different worlds but they can co-exist in a standoff. An
armistice exists between the incongrous worlds of farming and war as long as
forces which can destroy are directed towards creation, while Jupiter acts as
foreman on the celestial assembly line!

P.-H. Schrijvers, 'La valeur de la pitié chez Virgile (dans l'Enéide) et
chez quelques-uns de ses interprètes,' Présence, 483-95.
Basson
deals
with
Vergil's
conception
of
history
and
examines
A.
6.752-892; A. 1.257-96, and A. 8.626-731. Ward Briggs explores a hitherto
ill-defined area of association involving the Georgics and the epic, and provides new insights into the nature of the Vergilian simile. Brooks counters
Quinn's isolation of the heroic impulse to the Aeneid and finds its presence in
Homeric epic. Lee's study centers on 'the unavailing fathers and dutiful sons'
and on Vergil's melancholy vision of 'a failed humanity and a flawed universe.' The pattern of father-son relationships emerges as a dominant shaping

raw_text:
R. Schilling, 'Penatibus et Magnis Dis (Virgile, Énéide 3, 12 et 8, 679),'
Miscellanea di Studi Classici in onore di Eugenio Manni (Roma 1979).
The song of lopas, according to Kinsey, contains no symbolism, although
it plays an
important role
in
the development of the Aeneas-Dido
relationship. There
is also no attempt to arouse Roman prejudice against the
Carthaginians. Gaunt examines the bardic song of lopas which, inhis view,
fits the occasion, and suits the grandeur of the epic. The Cognys find a
rhetorical association for the motif of the severed-head on the river bank or
shore-line. Kenney's critical study of the similes in Aen. 2.469-505 introduces
Homer, Nicander, Lucretius, and the Georgics. He also explores the encircling
structures of 469-500 and the problem of ambiguity attaching to limen and
postes in 500-505. Zurli treats the Celaeno prophecy (A. 3.253-7). Farron
argues that Vergil deliberately portrays Aeneas' mission as brutal and destructive inAen. 4. Heuzé compares the agony of Dido and Camilla and comments
on the composition of the epic. Breaks with the oriental past and progress
towards a Roman future are, for Holt, the main theme of the Fifth Aeneid.
Bollók finds the inspiration for A. 6 inan Orphic Catabasis which is indebted
to the Egyptian Book of the Dead. Vergil may have discovered his model in
Epicurean polemics against popular beliefs. Martin examines A. 6.440-444,
and 450-66. Grimal studies A. 8.345-6, 347, 313. Monteleone is concerned
with the invocation to the Muse at the start of the lliadic section of the epic.
Erato introduces the impending epithalamium (Aeneas & Lavinia) and follows
neoteric patterns. Schilling is concerned with the Kabeiroi of Samothrace.
63
Mission and Rome,' G&R 27 (1980) 34-47.

R. Schilling, 'Penatibus et Magnis Dis (Virgile, Énéide 3, 12 et 8, 679),'
Miscellanea di Studi Classici in onore di Eugenio Manni (Roma 1979).
The song of lopas, according to Kinsey, contains no symbolism, although
it plays an
important role
in
the development of the Aeneas-Dido
relationship. There
is also no attempt to arouse Roman prejudice against the
Carthaginians. Gaunt examines the bardic song of lopas which, inhis view,
fits the occasion, and suits the grandeur of the epic. The Cognys find a
rhetorical association for the motif of the severed-head on the river bank or
shore-line. Kenney's critical study of the similes in Aen. 2.469-505 introduces
Homer, Nicander, Lucretius, and the Georgics. He also explores the encircling
structures of 469-500 and the problem of ambiguity attaching to limen and
postes in 500-505. Zurli treats the Celaeno prophecy (A. 3.253-7). Farron
argues that Vergil deliberately portrays Aeneas' mission as brutal and destructive inAen. 4. Heuzé compares the agony of Dido and Camilla and comments
on the composition of the epic. Breaks with the oriental past and progress
towards a Roman future are, for Holt, the main theme of the Fifth Aeneid.
Bollók finds the inspiration for A. 6 inan Orphic Catabasis which is indebted
to the Egyptian Book of the Dead. Vergil may have discovered his model in
Epicurean polemics against popular beliefs. Martin examines A. 6.440-444,
and 450-66. Grimal studies A. 8.345-6, 347, 313. Monteleone is concerned
with the invocation to the Muse at the start of the lliadic section of the epic.
Erato introduces the impending epithalamium (Aeneas & Lavinia) and follows
neoteric patterns. Schilling is concerned with the Kabeiroi of Samothrace.

raw_text:
M. Willumsen, 'Dido ambigua bilinguisque: For en nogten vurdering
af Vergils Didofigur,' MT 34-5 (1979) 72-5.
Barchiesi
finds analogies
between
the
Palinurus and Caieta accounts.
Berényi-Révész finds dementia wanting in Vergil's hero. Fides and amicitia
are the bonds of association between Aeneas and his companions. Their harmonious links (marked by the use of iaeti) characterise the later unanimity
between princeps, senate, and people. Elftmann notes the division between
youth and old age in the epic; Vergil's interest
in the lifetime and age of
individuals
is
remarkable.
Ross and
Trahmann
are both concerned
with
Ascanius who emerges as a pattern and ideal, a standard by which others may
measure and direct their conduct. Thill argues that the Roman national virtues of virtus, fides, aná pietas were present inHomer's Hector. Van den Berg
examines the character of Aeneas inhis speech (A. 4.333-61). West treats the
tragic careers of Anna and Juturna and finds the latter's tragedy more poignant. Pöschl's suggestion that the Aeneid is an epic of love where the deepest
tragedy
is that people 'loved too much' should properly include Anna and
Juturna whose careers are bound to one another through verbal parallels and
plot similarities.
105 J.-Chr. Billigmeier, 'Troy, Taruiša, and the Etruscans,' TALANTA
(Proc. Dutch Archaeol. and Hist. Soc.) 8-9 (1977) 5-10.

are the bonds of association between Aeneas and his companions. Their har-
monious links (marked by the use of iaeti) characterise the later unanimity
between princeps, senate, and people. Elftmann notes the division between
youth and old age in the epic; Vergil's interest
in the lifetime and age of
individuals
is
remarkable.
Ross and
Trahmann
are both concerned
with
Ascanius who emerges as a pattern and ideal, a standard by which others may
measure and direct their conduct. Thill argues that the Roman national vir-
tues of virtus, fides, aná pietas were present inHomer's Hector. Van den Berg
examines the character of Aeneas inhis speech (A. 4.333-61). West treats the
tragic careers of Anna and Juturna and finds the latter's tragedy more poig-
nant. Pöschl's suggestion that the Aeneid is an epic of love where the deepest
tragedy
is that people 'loved too much' should properly include Anna and
Juturna whose careers are bound to one another through verbal parallels and
plot similarities.

Holland's remarkable study includes Vergil in its intriguing compass. She
shows that Lucretius came
from
the same area of Northern
Italy
the
Transpadane - as Catullus and Vergil. She examines language-attitudes and
peculiarities of the period, the historical relations between poets from the
provinces and the urban audience in Rome, and the personal and political
connections of literary figures of the period. She also argues that the original
dedication of De Rerum Natura was to Julius Caesar.

raw_text:
Agathe Thornton,
The Living Universe: Gods and Men in
Virgil's
Aeneid (Leiden 1976). Rev:
N. Horsfall, JRS 69 (1979) 234; M. С. J.Putnam, CW 73 (1979) 40-41; G. B. Townsend, CR 29 (1979) 35-7.
Grimal finds analogies between the funerary rites of A. 5 and those still in
use among the Dogons. He argues that the funeral masks may have Ethiopian
prototypes. Thompson argues that the gods of the epic are entirely unnecessary for the unfolding of the story (so too, with Berlioz' Les Troyens).

raw_text:
Edwin A. Judge, 'Veni,
Vidi, Vici, and the Inscription of Cornelius
Gallus,' Akten des VI. International Kongress für Griechiche und Lateinische
66
Holland's remarkable study includes Vergil in its intriguing compass. She
shows that Lucretius came
from
the same area of Northern
Italy
the
Transpadane - as Catullus and Vergil. She examines language-attitudes and
peculiarities of the period, the historical relations between poets from the
provinces and the urban audience in Rome, and the personal and political
connections of literary figures of the period. She also argues that the original
dedication of De Rerum Natura was to Julius Caesar.

raw_text:
J. Van Sickle, 'Gallus and Callimachus:
parallel
texts,' LCM 5.5
(1980) 109.
The shadowy figure of Cornelius Gallus has come into sharper focus lately.
The fragmentary elegiacs discovered at Qasr Ibrîm have been submitted to
searching analysis by several scholars (131) and Vergil's links with the careeristpoet have claimed some attention (op. cit., 148-55). Judge's article introduces
the inscription at Philae (ILS 8995),
the Vatican obelisk, and the
Cleveland
portrait (?)
(cf. G. Grimm, JDA! 85 [1970]
158-70). Dorothy
Thompson's search
for an historical background for the Tazza Farnese
in
Naples leads to Actium. The spirit and detail of Vergil's description are similar;
there
is no place for rejoicing, only lamentation (cf. A. 8.707ff., and
680). Thompson suggests Gallan (and Octavian) associations and speculates
that Vergil may have seen a comparable tableau in the triumphal procession.
Lee suggests that the fragmentary elegiacs may be seen as parts of one complex poem rather than as separate epigrams, and Van Sickle compares the P.
Qa$r
Ibrîm lines with Callimachus Epigram 15(Pf.) where he detects comparable arrangement of words and other parallels. Boyancé argues that Horace
Epode 16 preceded Vergil Ed. 4. Saylor suggests that the storm of Horace c.
1, 2 may have found inspiration in the storm of Aeneid 1 (or Geòrgie 1).
Ferguson detects word play, imitation of Catullus and Vergil, golden lines,
rhyme, rhythms, and effects reminiscent of Vergil in Ovid's Metamorphoses.
Griffin argues
that Ovid's Metamorphoses, though
less well organised than
Vergil's epic, offers an equally valid interpretation of reality. Putnam argues
that
Propertius
measured
his thought in 3.22 against Geòrgie 2
(Laudes
Italiae) and that the moral dilemmas of the Aeneid may have been equally
important to Ovid. Rutledge finds similarities between Fasti 5.637-62, and
Aeneid 8.31-67.

title: ) 109. The shadowy figure of Cornelius Gallus has come into sharper focus lately. The fragmentary elegiacs discovered at Qasr Ibrîm have been submitted to searching analysis by several scholars (131) and Vergil's links with the careeristpoet have claimed some attention (op. cit., 148-55). Judge's article introduces the inscription at Philae (ILS 8995), the Vatican obelisk, and the Cleveland portrait (?) (cf. G. Grimm, JDA! 85 [
publication_block: 1970] 158-70). Dorothy Thompson's search for an historical background for the Tazza Farnese in Naples leads to Actium. The spirit and detail of Vergil's description are similar; there is no place for rejoicing, only lamentation (cf. A. 8.707ff., and 680). Thompson suggests Gallan (and Octavian) associations and speculates that Vergil may have seen a comparable tableau in the triumphal procession. Lee suggests that the fragmentary elegiacs may be seen as parts of one complex poem rather than as separate epigrams, and Van Sickle compares the P. Qa$r Ibrîm lines with Callimachus Epigram 15(Pf.) where he detects comparable arrangement of words and other parallels. Boyancé argues that Horace Epode 16 preceded Vergil Ed. 4. Saylor suggests that the storm of Horace c. 1, 2 may have found inspiration in the storm of Aeneid 1 (or Geòrgie 1). Ferguson detects word play, imitation of Catullus and Vergil, golden lines, rhyme, rhythms, and effects reminiscent of Vergil in Ovid's Metamorphoses. Griffin argues that Ovid's Metamorphoses, though less well organised than Vergil's epic, offers an equally valid interpretation of reality. Putnam argues that Propertius measured his thought in 3.22 against Geòrgie 2 (Laudes Italiae) and that the moral dilemmas of the Aeneid may have been equally important to Ovid. Rutledge finds similarities between Fasti 5.637-62, and Aeneid 8.31-67

raw_text:
J. Van Sickle, 'Gallus and Callimachus:
parallel
texts,' LCM 5.5
(1980) 109.
The shadowy figure of Cornelius Gallus has come into sharper focus lately.
The fragmentary elegiacs discovered at Qasr Ibrîm have been submitted to
searching analysis by several scholars (131) and Vergil's links with the careeristpoet have claimed some attention (op. cit., 148-55). Judge's article introduces
the inscription at Philae (ILS 8995),
the Vatican obelisk, and the
Cleveland
portrait (?)
(cf. G. Grimm, JDA! 85 [1970]
158-70). Dorothy
Thompson's search
for an historical background for the Tazza Farnese
in
Naples leads to Actium. The spirit and detail of Vergil's description are similar;
there
is no place for rejoicing, only lamentation (cf. A. 8.707ff., and
680). Thompson suggests Gallan (and Octavian) associations and speculates
that Vergil may have seen a comparable tableau in the triumphal procession.
Lee suggests that the fragmentary elegiacs may be seen as parts of one complex poem rather than as separate epigrams, and Van Sickle compares the P.
Qa$r
Ibrîm lines with Callimachus Epigram 15(Pf.) where he detects comparable arrangement of words and other parallels. Boyancé argues that Horace
Epode 16 preceded Vergil Ed. 4. Saylor suggests that the storm of Horace c.
1, 2 may have found inspiration in the storm of Aeneid 1 (or Geòrgie 1).
Ferguson detects word play, imitation of Catullus and Vergil, golden lines,
rhyme, rhythms, and effects reminiscent of Vergil in Ovid's Metamorphoses.
Griffin argues
that Ovid's Metamorphoses, though
less well organised than
Vergil's epic, offers an equally valid interpretation of reality. Putnam argues
that
Propertius
measured
his thought in 3.22 against Geòrgie 2
(Laudes
Italiae) and that the moral dilemmas of the Aeneid may have been equally
important to Ovid. Rutledge finds similarities between Fasti 5.637-62, and
Aeneid 8.31-67.

The fragmentary elegiacs discovered at Qasr Ibrîm have been submitted to searching analysis by several scholars (131) and Vergil's links with the careeristpoet have claimed some attention (op. cit., 148-55). Judge's article introduces the inscription at Philae (ILS 8995), the Vatican obelisk, and the Cleveland portrait (?) (cf. G. Grimm, JDA! 85 [
publication_block: 1970] 158-70). Dorothy Thompson's search for an historical background for the Tazza Farnese in Naples leads to Actium. The spirit and detail of Vergil's description are similar; there is no place for rejoicing, only lamentation (cf. A. 8.707ff., and 680). Thompson suggests Gallan (and Octavian) associations and speculates that Vergil may have seen a comparable tableau in the triumphal procession. Lee suggests that the fragmentary elegiacs may be seen as parts of one complex poem rather than as separate epigrams, and Van Sickle compares the P. raw_text:
A. J.Vaccaro, "Irregularidades y curiosidades en el hexámetro de las
Eglogas,,' EClás 20 (1 976) 9-44.
Ward
Briggs' note shows how Vergil adapts part of the structure of a
passage
which is distinctively
epic in tone,
the loss of Eurydice
(G. 4.
481-527), and uses it twice in passages describing Aeneas' interviews with
Venus, and with Creusa, inAeneid 2. Deliberate variation of obvious repetition is characteristic of Vergil. Edgeworth notes that Vergil never mentions
the colour of honey but refers eight times to its liquid state. He understood
what chloros meant (liquid, moist) and sought to reflect that meaning inhis
choice of words. Harrison's retort to Richmond's discussion of Vergil's use of
symbols is concerned most with A. 1.31 4ff, Aeneas' encounter with Venus
disguised as Artemis-Diana.
Richter-Thill notes that Vergil accents relations
between
earthly creatures and
the world that envelops them. Schmiel examines
the
resonances of Catullus
101
(Multas per gentes et multa per
aequora vectus) in Vergil.
Usages occur in contexts of travel, death, and
human
loss. Shea notes analogies between Lucretius 6.172-203, and Aen.
2.52-6, and 8.407-53. Vergil borrows and interprets the Lucretian passage
within the framework of the Aeolus myth. Thomas suggests that white and
red flowers normally presage death (poppies, lilies, etc). Jointly, or individually, the two colours indicate present or imminent death.
157.
G.
S.

e
contraddizioni
nel

dell'Eneide (Roma 1975).

Schmiel examines the resonances of Catullus 101 (Multas per gentes et multa per aequora vectus) in Vergil. Usages occur in contexts of travel, death, and human loss. Shea notes analogies between Lucretius 6.172-203, and Aen. 2.52-6, and 8.407-53. Vergil borrows and interprets the Lucretian passage within the framework of the Aeolus myth. Thomas suggests that white and red flowers normally presage death (poppies, lilies, etc). Jointly, or individually, the two colours indicate present or imminent death. 157. G. S. Baldini Moscadi assesses Pharsalia 6 as a response to Aeneid 6. Scaeva is a
negative version of the pius Aeneas, and Lucan's book ends with a scene of
necromancy,
the antithesis of Vergil's underworld account. Baldwin finds
Vergilianisms in the Historia Augusta. Szelest treats Vergil as a source for
Lucan.

P. Courcelle, 'Littérature latine d'époque chrétienne,' AEHE (Sect. 4)
70
Baldini Moscadi assesses Pharsalia 6 as a response to Aeneid 6. Scaeva is a
negative version of the pius Aeneas, and Lucan's book ends with a scene of
necromancy,
the antithesis of Vergil's underworld account. Baldwin finds
Vergilianisms in the Historia Augusta. Szelest treats Vergil as a source for
Lucan.

raw_text:
Susan Ford Wiltshire, 'Aeneas in America,' Vergilius 25 (1979) 2-9.
Mediaeval, Renaissance, Eighteenth Century and modern uses and translations of Vergil are prime subject-matter in the Caesarodunum volume edited
by Chevallier. Cadoni's article treating De compendiosa doctrina advances
variant readings for Vergil's works. Courcelle (194) probes the Christian interpretation of A. 7.338 mille nocendi artes and (195) the literary posterity of
A. 2.1-338. Franchey d'Espèrey treats the wounding of Silvia's stag as archetype for comparable incidents inStatius, Valerius Flaccus, and Sřlius Italicus.
Doignon finds reminiscences of Vergil and Seneca in Lactantius. Drake and
Forbes establish
that Vida's Christiad was token of the piety of the High
Renaissance, the metamorphosis of the Vulgate into a Vergilian counterpart,
a marriage of Classical and Biblical styles and forms. Ebeling-Koning studies
the influence of Vergil and Prudentius on Waltharius. Epstein's verses on
Virgil are noteworthy. Feo's article sheds light on Petrarch's doubts about the
Homeric and Vergilian Hades. Friedrich examines Servius ad Aen. 12.605 and
Mosella 316. Huebner studies G. 1.33-5 and Claudian. McGaha notes that
Cervantes' Don Quixote eliminated the flaws which diminished the credibility
of Vergil's epic: the reliance on magic, and divine machinery. Cervantes Christianized the Roman doctrine of fate and created a 'modern' hero, not the
plaster saint which mediaeval and Renaissance commentators had made of
Aeneas. Nazzaro argues that Pope Damasius used Vergil's Eclogue less
in
sympathy than in conflict. The ideology and literary values of Vergil were
adverse
to
Christian notions. Volta used
Lucretian and Vergilian tags
in
editing his scientific treatises, according to Pasoli. Poirion underscores the
ideological transformation of Vergil in the Roman d'Énéas. Allegory eased
the adaptation
to new political, social, and aesthetic conditions. Reinhold
examines Robert Lowell's distortion of Vergil's Second Aeneid; he assigns the
role of Aeneas to Ulysses as narrator of the fall of Troy. Magna pars fui, says
Vergil's hero; 'He had his part' says Lowell of Ulysses! Ricci studies a Vergilian cento from the standpoint of theme, structure, genre, meaning, and text.
Rudat's essays highlight the destruction of the epic poem and the epic hero.
Thompson notices a classical allusion (Quos ego . . .) in Chapter One of Flaubert's Madame Bovary. Susan Wiltshire explores the myth of Old Troy in the
American tradition: novels by Jo Reinhard Smith and Laura Krey, and works
by Allen Tate and Robert Penn Warren. Homecomings are paramount: 'the
homecoming is a problematic one . . . and as perilous as life is - but itis this
journey that defines Vergil's poem as the epic of civilization'.
McMaster University
Hamilton, Canada
A. G. McKay

title: Susan Ford Wiltshire, 'Aeneas in America,' Vergilius 25 (
publication_block: 1979) 2-9. Mediaeval, Renaissance, Eighteenth Century and modern uses and translations of Vergil are prime subject-matter in the Caesarodunum volume edited by Chevallier. Cadoni's article treating De compendiosa doctrina advances variant readings for Vergil's works. Courcelle (194) probes the Christian interpretation of A. 7.338 mille nocendi artes and (195) the literary posterity of A. 2.1-338. Franchey d'Espèrey treats the wounding of Silvia's stag as archetype for comparable incidents inStatius, Valerius Flaccus, and Sřlius Italicus. Doignon finds reminiscences of Vergil and Seneca in Lactantius. Drake and Forbes establish that Vida's Christiad was token of the piety of the High Renaissance, the metamorphosis of the Vulgate into a Vergilian counterpart, a marriage of Classical and Biblical styles and forms. Ebeling-Koning studies the influence of Vergil and Prudentius on Waltharius. Epstein's verses on Virgil are noteworthy. Feo's article sheds light on Petrarch's doubts about the Homeric and Vergilian Hades. Friedrich examines Servius ad Aen. 12.605 and Mosella 316. Huebner studies G. 1.33-5 and Claudian. McGaha notes that Cervantes' Don Quixote eliminated the flaws which diminished the credibility of Vergil's epic: the reliance on magic, and divine machinery. Cervantes Christianized the Roman doctrine of fate and created a 'modern' hero, not the plaster saint which mediaeval and Renaissance commentators had made of Aeneas. Nazzaro argues that Pope Damasius used Vergil's Eclogue less in sympathy than in conflict. The ideology and literary values of Vergil were adverse to Christian notions. Volta used Lucretian and Vergilian tags in editing his scientific treatises, according to Pasoli. Poirion underscores the ideological transformation of Vergil in the Roman d'Énéas. Allegory eased the adaptation to new political, social, and aesthetic conditions. Reinhold examines Robert Lowell's distortion of Vergil's Second Aeneid; he assigns the role of Aeneas to Ulysses as narrator of the fall of Troy. Magna pars fui, says Vergil's hero; 'He had his part' says Lowell of Ulysses! Ricci studies a Vergilian cento from the standpoint of theme, structure, genre, meaning, and text. Rudat's essays highlight the destruction of the epic poem and the epic hero. Thompson notices a classical allusion (Quos ego . . .) in Chapter One of Flaubert's Madame Bovary. Susan Wiltshire explores the myth of Old Troy in the American tradition: novels by Jo Reinhard Smith and Laura Krey, and works by Allen Tate and Robert Penn Warren. Homecomings are paramount: 'the homecoming is a problematic one . . . and as perilous as life is - but itis this journey that defines Vergil's poem as the epic of civilization'.
